metadata.career_relevance: Pattern matching powers Zerodha's trade log search, Flipkart's product search, and every text editor's Ctrl+F. The naive O(nm) fails for large texts. KMP O(n+m) is the canonical interview algorithm for 'advanced' string rounds at FAANG India.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha trade log search:
  Log: 10 million chars (daily trade records)
  Pattern: 'TIMEOUT_ERROR' (13 chars)

Naive search O(n×m): 10M × 13 = 130M comparisons
KMP O(n+m): 10M + 13 = ~10M comparisons

KMP is 13× faster for this pattern.
For repeated patterns: KMP 100× faster or more.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Full KMP implementation: LPS array + search. Then use it to solve real string matching problems.

visual_aid.description: KMP diagram. Top: LPS array construction for 'AABAAB': table showing index, char, prefix-suffix matches, lps value. Bottom: KMP search on text='AABAABAAB', pattern='AABAAB'. Show how mismatch at j=5 jumps to j=lps[4]=2 instead of restarting at i+1. Mark: 'i stays, j jumps back via LPS — never re-examine text chars'. Final: match found at position 3.
visual_aid.alt_text: KMP LPS array and search with pointer jumps

worked_example.problem_statement: Return all starting indices where pattern occurs in text.

step_by_step_solution.details: lps = [0,1,0,1,2,3]. At each position: what's the longest prefix that equals the suffix of pattern[0..i]? This tells us: on mismatch, how far back to jump in the pattern (not the text).
i=text index, j=pattern index. text[i]==pattern[j]: match, both advance. Mismatch with j>0: j=lps[j-1], i stays. Mismatch with j==0: i++. Match complete (j==m): record position i-m, j=lps[j-1].

worked_example.expected_output:
Matches at indices: [0, 3]  (0-indexed)
Verify: text[0..5]='AABAAB' ✓ text[3..8]='AABAAB' ✓
things_to_notice: KMP never backtracks the text pointer i — it only moves forward. This guarantees O(n) text processing.
The LPS array encodes the 'skip' information for the pattern. Building it takes O(m). Combined: O(n+m).
When a match completes, j resets to lps[m-1] (not 0) — overlapping matches are correctly handled.
lps[0] is always 0 (no proper prefix of a single char). lps[j] = 0 means 'no useful skip, start over from pattern beginning'.

industry_examples.use_case: Search 10GB of daily trade logs for error patterns. KMP: O(n) per pattern. Without KMP: O(n×m) — for m=20 char pattern and n=10⁹ text chars, 10× slower. Real usage: grep, text editors, database query engines all use variants of KMP or Boyer-Moore.

applications.interview_relevance: KMP is a 'senior/advanced' string interview question. Correctly implementing LPS array + search within 20 minutes signals strong algorithm knowledge. Start by explaining the motivation (O(nm) naive fails), then describe LPS conceptually, then code it.
applications.real_world_workflow: Use Java's built-in String.indexOf() for most tasks (internally optimized). Only implement KMP manually when: (1) asked in interview, (2) need to find ALL occurrences with overlaps, (3) text is streamed and can't be loaded fully.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Building LPS: confusing the comparison. When pattern[len] == pattern[i]: lps[i] = len+1, len++, i++. When mismatch with len>0: len = lps[len-1] (don't reset to 0 immediately). When mismatch with len==0: lps[i]=0, i++.
common_gaps.remediation: Trace LPS construction character by character on paper first. lps[i] = length of longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also its suffix. 'Proper' means it can't be the whole string.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student's LPS array is off by one or has wrong values for repeated patterns like 'AAAA'

key_takeaways: Naive pattern matching: O(nm) — fails for large texts or repeated chars in pattern
KMP: O(n+m) — build LPS array O(m), then search O(n)
LPS[i]: length of longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that equals its suffix
KMP key insight: on mismatch, use LPS to skip — text pointer i never goes back
On complete match: j = lps[m-1] (handle overlapping matches)
For interviews: explain motivation → LPS concept → code LPS → code search
Java's String.indexOf(): use in production (optimized); implement KMP for interviews

self_check_questions.question: Why does KMP never move the text pointer backwards?
self_check_questions.answer: The LPS (failure function) tells us that the prefix of the pattern we just matched also appears as a suffix of the same matched region. So when a mismatch occurs, we don't need to re-check text characters we already matched — we already know what they are (they matched the pattern prefix, which equals the LPS suffix). We reset j to lps[j-1] to use this knowledge: we're saying 'the last lps[j-1] characters of what we matched still match the beginning of the pattern.' The text pointer i can always stay put or advance — never retreating.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Palindromes — checking and finding palindromic substrings in O(n).
